abstract: Recent advances in large language model (LLM) have empowered autonomous agents to perform complex tasks that require multi-turn interactions with tools and environments. However, scaling such agent training is limited by the lack of diverse and reliable environments. In this paper, we propose Agent World Model (AWM), a fully synthetic environment generation pipeline. Using this pipeline, we scale to 1,000 environments covering everyday scenarios, in which agents can interact with rich toolsets (35 tools per environment on average) and obtain high-quality observations. Notably, these environments are code-driven and backed by databases, providing more reliable and consistent state transitions than environments simulated by LLMs. Moreover, they enable more efficient agent interaction compared with collecting trajectories from realistic environments. To demonstrate the effectiveness of this resource, we perform large-scale reinforcement learning for multi-turn tool-use agents. Thanks to the fully executable environments and accessible database states, we can also design reliable reward functions. Experiments on three benchmarks show that training exclusively in synthetic environments, rather than benchmark-specific ones, yields strong out-of-distribution generalization. The code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

keyInsights: Code-driven and database-backed synthetic environments provide superior reliability and state consistency compared to purely LLM-simulated ones, which is crucial for effective and scalable reinforcement learning.

An iterative, execution-based self-correction loop is highly effective in enhancing the quality and robustness of LLM-generated code and environment components, achieving over 85% first-attempt success rates.

The 'code-augmented LLM-as-a-Judge' offers a more robust reward signal by effectively balancing the precision of code-based verification with the contextual understanding of LLMs, outperforming singular approaches.
originalProblem: A severe scarcity of diverse, reliable, and efficient training environments hinders the scalable development of LLM-powered autonomous agents via reinforcement learning.

Existing real-world environments are expensive and lack stability, while human-crafted environments are limited in scale and diversity; LLM-simulated environments suffer from unreliability due to hallucinations and high inference costs.
A need for consistent state transitions and efficient interaction capabilities that can support thousands of reinforcement learning training steps.
results: AWM successfully synthesized 1,000 unique environments with an average of 35 tools each (totaling 35,062 tools) and 10,000 diverse tasks, establishing the largest open-source tool-use environment set to date.

Agents trained exclusively on AWM's synthetic environments consistently outperformed baseline LLMs and agents trained in LLM-simulated environments across three distinct, out-of-distribution real-world benchmarks (BFCLv3, τ2-bench, MCP-Universe).

The study demonstrated that increasing the scale and diversity of training environments leads to a monotonic improvement in agent performance and generalization capabilities, validating the importance of large-scale synthetic data.
solution: Developed the Agent World Model (AWM) pipeline, which uses LLMs to synthesize 1,000 executable, code-driven tool-use environments with database-backed (SQLite) state, complete with diverse scenarios, tasks, and Python backend code.

Integrated an execution-based self-correction mechanism that iteratively feeds back compilation and runtime errors to the LLM for refinement of synthesized database schemas, interface code, and verification functions.

Implemented a 'code-augmented LLM-as-a-Judge' verification module that combines structured, code-based state checks with LLM reasoning to provide robust and reliable reward signals for reinforcement learning.
paper_summary.summary: The Agent World Model (AWM) introduces an open-source, code-driven pipeline that synthesizes 1,000 diverse, executable tool-use environments with database-backed state for large-scale reinforcement learning of autonomous agents. Agents trained on these synthetic environments, developed collaboratively by UNC Chapel Hill and Snowflake, demonstrate strong out-of-distribution generalization across various real-world benchmarks, validating the approach for developing robust LLM agents.

title: Agent World Model: Infinity Synthetic Environments for Agentic Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning increasingly relies on experience-driven scaling, yet real-world environments remain non-adaptive, limited in coverage, and difficult to scale. World models offer a potential way to improve learning efficiency through simulated experience, but it remains unclear whether large language models can reliably serve this role and under what conditions they meaningfully benefit agents. We study these questions in text-based environments, which provide a controlled setting to reinterpret language modeling as next-state prediction under interaction. We introduce a three-level framework for evaluating LLM-based world models: (i) fidelity and consistency, (ii) scalability and robustness, and (iii) agent utility. Across five representative environments, we find that sufficiently trained world models maintain coherent latent state, scale predictably with data and model size, and improve agent performance via action verification, synthetic trajectory generation, and warm-starting reinforcement learning. Meanwhile, these gains depend critically on behavioral coverage and environment complexity, delineating clear boundry on when world modeling effectively supports agent learning.

organization_info.name: Southern University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Supervised fine-tuning dramatically improves LLM fidelity and consistency as world models, significantly outperforming prompting-based approaches for next-state prediction.

LLM-based world models demonstrate predictable scaling with data and model size, show robustness to out-of-distribution settings, and benefit from cross-environment training and diverse behavioral coverage.

These world models offer practical utility for agents by enabling action verification, generating high-quality synthetic training data, and providing effective early experience for policy warm-starting.
originalProblem: Agentic reinforcement learning faces an 'experience bottleneck' due to the high cost, non-adaptivity, and limited coverage of real-world environment interactions.
Prior LLM-based world modeling efforts were limited by structured state representations, reliance on low-accuracy prompting methods, and insufficient evaluation of long-horizon consistency.
A fundamental question remained regarding whether LLMs, primarily trained for next-token prediction, could reliably and consistently predict next states in interactive environments.
results: Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B and Llama-3.1-8B achieved exact match accuracies of up to 99.87% in ALFWorld and 98.60% in SciWorld for one-step prediction.
World models maintained high consistency ratios for multi-step rollouts in structured environments (e.g., ALFWorld: 96%, SciWorld: 91%, TextWorld: 92%), indicating reliable long-horizon simulation.
Utilizing world models for action verification or synthetic data generation led to higher agent task success rates and improved learning efficiency compared to baseline methods.
solution: The authors formalized world modeling as a multi-turn natural language simulation task, where LLMs generate next-state transitions in free text to support general and compositional interactions.
They employed supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on large-scale multi-turn interaction trajectories collected from GPT-4o across five diverse text-based environments (e.g., ALFWorld, WebShop).
A three-level evaluation framework was established to rigorously assess LLM-based world models across fidelity, consistency, scalability, robustness, and practical agent utility.
paper_summary.summary: This research systematically explores whether large language models (LLMs) can serve as implicit text-based world models for agents, demonstrating that supervised fine-tuning enables them to reliably simulate multi-turn interactions. Fine-tuned LLMs achieved up to 99.87% exact match accuracy in structured environments and over 90% consistency for multi-step rollouts, showing utility in action verification, synthetic data generation, and policy warm-starting.

title: From Word to World: Can Large Language Models be Implicit Text-based World Models?

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are transforming automated program repair (APR) through agent-based approaches that localize bugs, generate patches, and verify fixes. However, the lack of high-quality, scalable training datasets, especially those with verifiable outputs and intermediate reasoning traces-limits progress, particularly for open-source models. In this work, we present SWE-Synth, a framework for synthesizing realistic, verifiable, and process-aware bug-fix datasets at the repository level. SWE-Synth leverages LLM agents to simulate debugging workflows, producing not only bug-fix pairs but also test cases and structured repair trajectories. Compared to manually curated datasets, our method scales with minimal human effort while preserving contextual richness and correctness. Experiments show that models trained on SWE-Synth outperform those trained on real-world datasets by 2.3% on SWE-Bench Lite. Our results highlight the potential of synthetic, agent-generated data to advance the state of the art in APR and software engineering automation.

keyInsights: Training on successful rollout trajectories, which include intermediate repair steps and process-aware data, is crucial for effectively teaching LLMs iterative debugging strategies.
Prioritizing component selection based on test coverage during bug generation yields more challenging and valuable synthetic bugs that lead to improved APR model performance.

Synthetic bug-fix data, when generated with realism and verifiability, can effectively scale and significantly improve LLM performance in APR tasks, proving difficult for human developers to distinguish from real bugs.
originalProblem: A lack of high-quality, scalable training data for LLMs in Automated Program Repair (APR), particularly for open-source models, hinders their ability to resolve real-world bugs.
Existing bug-fix datasets from open-source repositories are often noisy, lack intermediate reasoning traces, and rarely include test-based verifiability.
Traditional synthetic data generation methods often produce trivial or unrealistic bugs, limiting their utility for training sophisticated LLM agents for complex debugging.
results: Models trained on SWE-Synth achieved a 15.3% resolve rate on SWE-Bench Lite, outperforming models trained on SWE-Gym Lite (12.0%) and SWE-Gym Full (13.0%).

A human study involving 11 Python-experienced participants revealed an overall accuracy of 55.17% in distinguishing real from synthetic bugs, with synthetic bugs more frequently misclassified as real.

Training on successful rollout trajectories (SWE-Synth's approach) yielded a 15.33% resolve rate, significantly outperforming a 'naive reverse patch diff' strategy (0.67%) and RAG-based methods (3.00%).
solution: SWE-Synth, an LLM-agent-driven framework, synthesizes repository-level bug-fix datasets that are verifiable and process-aware through a multi-phase approach.
It employs LLM-based variant synthesis to generate human-like bugs, utilizing test coverage-based component selection to prioritize impactful code sections for re-implementation.

Ground-truth fixes are extracted by training an LLM agent on successful rollout trajectories, capturing iterative repair actions and error logs, providing rich supervisory signals beyond simple diffs.
paper_summary.summary: SWE-Synth introduces a framework for synthesizing verifiable, process-aware bug-fix datasets at the repository level, enabling Large Language Models to learn iterative debugging strategies. Models fine-tuned on SWE-Synth data achieved a 15.3% resolve rate on SWE-Bench Lite, surpassing performance from manually curated datasets, with synthetic bugs often indistinguishable from real ones by human developers.

title: SWE-Synth: Synthesizing Verifiable Bug-Fix Data to Enable Large Language Models in Resolving Real-World Bugs

abstract: LLM agents excel in compact environments requiring deep reasoning but remain brittle when operating in broader, more complex contexts that demand robustness across diverse tools and schemas. Building bespoke environments for training is heavy, brittle, and limits progress. In this paper, we demonstrate that LLMs can simulate realistic environment feedback without access to actual testbed data or APIs. Inspired by this capability, we propose two frameworks: Simia-SFT, a pipeline that synthesizes SFT data by amplifying small seed sets into diverse trajectories in an environment-agnostic manner, and Simia-RL, a framework that enables RL training without real environment implementations through LLM-simulated feedback. Fine-tuning open models yields consistent improvements across multiple benchmarks, surpassing GPT-4o and approaching o4-mini on $\tau^2$-Bench. Together, Simia-SFT and Simia-RL enable scalable agent training without environment engineering, replacing heavy and brittle implementations with flexible LLM-based simulation.

keyInsights: LLMs possess inherent "world modeling abilities" that can be effectively exploited to simulate realistic environment feedback for agent training.
Replacing laborious environment engineering with LLM-based simulation offers a scalable, accessible, and robust paradigm for developing LLM agents.
LLM-simulated environments can provide richer, more detailed, and adaptive feedback, which is crucial for agent learning, especially in reinforcement learning settings.
originalProblem: LLM agents are brittle and costly to develop for complex, real-world environments, often succeeding only in "complex-task/simple-environment" settings.
Traditional agent training relies on building bespoke environments with explicit tool interfaces, which is heavy, brittle, and limits progress and scalability.
Existing synthetic data generation methods are heavily environment-dependent, often requiring explicit environment implementations or relying on existing tool specifications.
results: Models fine-tuned using Simia-Tau consistently outperformed baselines; Simia-Tau (Qwen2.5-32B) achieved an average score of 58.9 on τ²-Bench, surpassing GPT-4o (54.2).
Simia-OB (Qwen3-8B) scored 44.0 on OfficeBench, significantly outperforming GPT-4 (31.1) by 12.9 points and improving over its seed-tuned counterpart by 14.4 points.
RL on simulated environments (e.g., Simia-OB-RL) yielded performance gains over SFT-only models and provided more informative feedback, enabling better error recovery and task success.
solution: Leveraging LLMs themselves as environment simulators to generate coherent environment dynamics, state transitions, and tool interactions without actual testbed data or APIs.
Developing Simia-SFT, an end-to-end, environment-agnostic pipeline that synthesizes diverse, multi-turn agent trajectories for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) data from small seed sets.

Introducing Simia-RL, an agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework where an LLM acts as both the environment feedback provider and the reward computation mechanism, allowing RL training without real environment implementations.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces a method for training large language model (LLM) agents using other LLMs as environment simulators, achieving up to 14.4 point average score improvements on benchmarks like OfficeBench for open models compared to seed-tuned baselines. This approach enables scalable generation of high-quality training data and facilitates reinforcement learning without dedicated environment engineering.

title: Simulating Environments with Reasoning Models for Agent Training

abstract: We propose SWE-Universe, a scalable and efficient framework for automatically constructing real-world software engineering (SWE) verifiable environments from GitHub pull requests (PRs). To overcome the prevalent challenges of automatic building, such as low production yield, weak verifiers, and prohibitive cost, our framework utilizes a building agent powered by an efficient custom-trained model. This agent employs iterative self-verification and in-loop hacking detection to ensure the reliable generation of high-fidelity, verifiable tasks. Using this method, we scale the number of real-world multilingual SWE environments to a million scale (807,693). We demonstrate the profound value of our environments through large-scale agentic mid-training and reinforcement learning. Finally, we applied this technique to Qwen3-Max-Thinking and achieved a score of 75.3% on SWE-Bench Verified. Our work provides both a critical resource and a robust methodology to advance the next generation of coding agents.

keyInsights: Autonomous agents with iterative self-correction and integrated hacking detection can reliably build high-fidelity, executable software engineering environments at an unprecedented scale.
Task-specific fine-tuning of a lightweight LLM (Qwen-Next-80A3) significantly boosts efficiency and verifier quality, outperforming larger general-purpose models in environment construction.

Access to a massive, multilingual dataset of real-world verifiable tasks is critical for advancing coding agents' cross-lingual generalization and achieving state-of-the-art performance on complex software engineering benchmarks.
originalProblem: Existing methods for generating software engineering (SWE) environments suffer from low production yield due to the complexity and heterogeneity of real-world repositories.
The quality of verifiers is often weak, allowing superficial solutions to pass without genuinely executing code, leading to unreliable training signals.

The cost and inefficiency of using large language models (LLMs) for dependency resolution and configuration make large-scale (million-scale) environment generation economically unsustainable and slow.
results: The framework successfully generated 807,693 multilingual, verifiable training instances from over 52,000 unique GitHub repositories, creating the largest and most diverse dataset of its kind.

The iterative validation loop increased the environment-building success rate from 82.6% to 94%, and the custom builder model achieved a 78.44% Success Rate (w/o Hack) on a diverse benchmark, surpassing Claude-Opus-4.5.

Training LLM agents with SWE-Universe data improved the Qwen3-Next-80A3 model's SWE-Bench Multilingual performance by 15 percentage points (to over 46%) and contributed to the flagship Qwen3-Max-Thinking model achieving a state-of-the-art 75.3% on SWE-Bench Verified.
solution: The SWE-Universe framework employs an autonomous building agent, powered by a custom-trained Qwen-Next-80A3 model, that iteratively generates and validates `evaluation.sh` scripts to reliably distinguish between buggy and fixed repository states.

An integrated 'Hacking Detector' module actively checks generated verifiers for superficial solutions (e.g., string matching) within the agent's work loop, forcing the generation of genuinely executable verification scripts.

A highly efficient architecture utilizing a lightweight Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLM and a distributed execution system (MEGAFLOW on Alibaba Cloud) enables cost-effective and concurrent processing of millions of GitHub pull requests.
paper_summary.summary: Alibaba Group's Qwen Team developed SWE-Universe, a framework to automatically construct over 800,000 real-world, multilingual, verifiable software engineering environments from GitHub PRs. This massive dataset significantly enhances coding agent capabilities, with the Qwen3-Max-Thinking model achieving 75.3% accuracy on SWE-Bench Verified and substantial gains on multilingual benchmarks.

title: SWE-Universe: Scale Real-World Verifiable Environments to Millions

abstract:
We introduce MLE-Dojo, a Gym-style framework for systematically reinforcement
learning, evaluating, and improving autonomous large language model (LLM)
agents in iterative machine learning engineering (MLE) workflows. Unlike
existing benchmarks that primarily rely on static datasets or single-attempt
evaluations, MLE-Dojo provides an interactive environment enabling agents to
iteratively experiment, debug, and refine solutions through structured feedback
loops. Built upon 200+ real-world Kaggle challenges, MLE-Dojo covers diverse,
open-ended MLE tasks carefully curated to reflect realistic engineering
scenarios such as data processing, architecture search, hyperparameter tuning,
and code debugging. Its fully executable environment supports comprehensive
agent training via both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning,
facilitating iterative experimentation, realistic data sampling, and real-time
outcome verification. Extensive evaluations of eight frontier LLMs reveal that
while current models achieve meaningful iterative improvements, they still
exhibit significant limitations in autonomously generating long-horizon
solutions and efficiently resolving complex errors. Furthermore, MLE-Dojo's
flexible and extensible architecture seamlessly integrates diverse data
sources, tools, and evaluation protocols, uniquely enabling model-based agent
tuning and promoting interoperability, scalability, and reproducibility. We
open-source our framework and benchmarks to foster community-driven innovation
towards next-generation MLE agents.

keyInsights: Current LLMs can achieve meaningful iterative improvements but struggle with long-horizon solutions and complex error resolution
Different models exhibit varying strategies in balancing code execution vs validation actions
Reasoning-focused models (e.g. o3-mini, DeepSeek-r1) consistently outperform non-reasoning models across metrics
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks lack interactive environments that support iterative experimentation and realistic ML engineering workflows
Current evaluation methods don't capture the continuous nature of ML development involving experimentation, debugging and feedback incorporation
results: Successfully enabled iterative improvement of LLM agents on real ML engineering tasks through interactive experimentation
Created public leaderboard comparing 8 frontier LLMs and multiple agent scaffolds
Demonstrated diverse solution strategies across models while identifying key limitations in autonomous capabilities
Provided reproducible platform for developing and evaluating ML engineering agents
solution: Created Gym-style interactive environments based on curated Kaggle competitions spanning multiple ML domains
Implemented standardized interfaces for observation/action spaces and modular architecture supporting diverse data sources and evaluation protocols
Developed comprehensive reward mechanisms reflecting solution quality using competition metrics
paper_summary.summary: A framework called MLE-Dojo enables training and evaluation of LLM-based agents on machine learning engineering tasks through interactive environments built from 200+ Kaggle competitions, providing standardized interfaces for observation, action, and reward spaces while supporting both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning approaches.

title: MLE-Dojo: Interactive Environments for Empowering LLM Agents in Machine  Learning Engineering

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are expected to be trained to act as agents in various real-world environments, but this process relies on rich and varied tool-interaction sandboxes. However, access to real systems is often restricted; LLM-simulated environments are prone to hallucinations and inconsistencies; and manually built sandboxes are hard to scale. In this paper, we propose EnvScaler, an automated framework for scalable tool-interaction environments via programmatic synthesis. EnvScaler comprises two components. First, SkelBuilder constructs diverse environment skeletons through topic mining, logic modeling, and quality evaluation. Then, ScenGenerator generates multiple task scenarios and rule-based trajectory validation functions for each environment. With EnvScaler, we synthesize 191 environments and about 7K scenarios, and apply them to Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Qwen3 series models. Results on three benchmarks show that EnvScaler significantly improves LLMs' ability to solve tasks in complex environments involving multi-turn, multi-tool interactions. We release our code and data at this https URL.

keyInsights: Automated programmatic synthesis driven by LLMs can generate a large scale of high-quality, executable, tool-interactive environments without relying on environmental priors or pre-collected toolsets.

Integrating a dual-agent assessment loop during environment skeleton synthesis is an effective method for ensuring the quality, correctness, and adherence to expected behaviors of generated tool implementations.

Rule-based, terminal-state validation functions provide a robust and precise reward signal for LLM agents, enabling effective policy optimization in reinforcement learning through partial credit for task completion.
originalProblem: A scarcity of diverse, scalable, and high-quality tool-interactive environments hinders the effective training of LLM agents.

Existing environments for LLM agents suffer limitations: real-world environments lack scalability, LLM-simulated environments are prone to hallucinations and inconsistencies, and manually built sandboxes are labor-intensive and not scalable for extensive training.
There is a need for automated mechanisms for quality assessment and robust, rule-based evaluation functions for complex, multi-turn agent interactions within environments.
results: EnvScaler successfully synthesized 191 distinct environments and approximately 7,000 diverse task scenarios, each averaging 18.58 tools and 21.38 state categories, demonstrating high complexity and richness.

Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with EnvScaler environments improved Qwen3 series models' performance by an average of 8.67 points on BFCL-MT, 4.29 points on Tau-Bench, and 11.57 points on ACEBench-Agent.

Reinforcement Learning (RL) further enhanced agent capabilities, with Qwen3-8B gaining an additional 4.88 points on BFCL-MT and 3.46 points on Tau-Bench, indicating the efficacy of the state-checking reward signals.
solution: EnvScaler employs a two-component framework: SkelBuilder, which constructs diverse environment skeletons, and ScenGenerator, which populates these skeletons with task scenarios.

SkelBuilder automates the synthesis of executable environment programs, documentation, and tool interfaces using task-guided environment discovery, a multi-stage program construction pipeline, and a dual-agent quality assessment loop.

ScenGenerator generates specific task scenarios for each environment, including initial state data, challenging tasks, and rule-based terminal-state validation functions for process-agnostic trajectory verification and reward computation.
paper_summary.summary: EnvScaler, developed by researchers at Renmin University of China, is an automated framework for synthesizing diverse, executable, tool-interactive environments for training large language model (LLM) agents. The system successfully generated 191 distinct environments and 7,000 task scenarios, which led to an average performance improvement of 8.67 points on BFCL-MT and 11.57 points on ACEBench-Agent for Qwen3 series models after Supervised Fine-Tuning.

title: EnvScaler: Scaling Tool-Interactive Environments for LLM Agent via Programmatic Synthesis

abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) to reason via reinforcement learning (RL) significantly improves their problem-solving capabilities. In agentic settings, existing methods like ReAct prompt LLMs to explicitly plan before every action; however, we demonstrate that always planning is computationally expensive and degrades performance on long-horizon tasks, while never planning further limits performance. To address this, we introduce a conceptual framework formalizing dynamic planning for LLM agents, enabling them to flexibly decide when to allocate test-time compute for planning. We propose a simple two-stage training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on diverse synthetic data to prime models for dynamic planning, and (2) RL to refine this capability in long-horizon environments. Experiments on the Crafter environment show that dynamic planning agents trained with this approach are more sample-efficient and consistently achieve more complex objectives. Additionally, we demonstrate that these agents can be effectively steered by human-written plans, surpassing their independent capabilities and highlighting the potential for safer and more collaborative agentic systems.
title: Learning When to Plan: Efficiently Allocating Test-Time Compute for LLM Agents

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents show great promise for complex, multi-turn tool-use tasks, but their development is often hampered by the extreme scarcity of high-quality training data. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on synthetic data leads to overfitting, whereas standard reinforcement learning (RL) struggles with a critical cold-start problem and training instability. To address these challenges, we introduce $\textbf{Environment Tuning}$, a novel training paradigm that enables agents to learn complex behaviors directly from problem instances without relying on pre-collected expert trajectories. $\textbf{Environment Tuning}$ orchestrates this learning process through a structured curriculum, actionable environment augmentation that provides corrective feedback, and fine-grained progress rewards to ensure stable and efficient exploration. Using only 400 problem instances from Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) benchmark, our method not only achieves competitive in-distribution performance against strong baselines but also demonstrates superior out-of-distribution generalization, overcoming the performance collapse common to SFT-based approaches. Our work presents a paradigm shift from supervised fine-tuning on static trajectories to dynamic, environment-based exploration, paving the way for training more robust and data-efficient agents. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Tuning the learning environment through structured feedback and progressive curricula is more effective for training robust LLM agents than solely fine-tuning the agent on static data.

Combining curriculum learning with actionable environment augmentation and dense rewards significantly stabilizes reinforcement learning, preventing common issues like catastrophic forgetting and gradient explosion in complex multi-turn tasks.

Providing targeted, corrective feedback within the environment allows agents to infer inter-tool dependencies and internal tool constraints dynamically, fostering better generalization to unseen scenarios.
originalProblem: Developing LLM agents for complex, multi-turn tool-use tasks faces data scarcity, as high-quality human-annotated datasets are rare and labor-intensive to create.
Existing supervised fine-tuning (SFT) methods often lead to agents that overfit static data and fail to generalize effectively to out-of-distribution scenarios.
Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) struggles with multi-turn tasks due to complex environments, the 'cold-start' problem, long interaction chains, sparse rewards, and training instability.
results: On the BFCL V3 multi-turn benchmark, ENVIRONMENT TUNING boosted Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct from 7.00% to 36.92%, surpassing proprietary models like Gemini 2.5 Pro and demonstrating superior performance over direct RL baselines.

The method substantially enhanced out-of-distribution generalization, with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct's Web Search score improving from 1.00% to 15.00% on BFCL V4, while SFT baselines often exhibited performance collapse.

Ablation studies confirmed the necessity of each component: the structured curriculum increased performance by 19.50% over direct GRPO, actionable environment augmentation provided over 20% improvement in challenging scenarios, and fine-grained rewards enabled stable learning where binary rewards led to training failure.
solution: ENVIRONMENT TUNING orchestrates agent learning through a four-stage structured curriculum that progressively builds skills from syntactic correctness to advanced problem-solving.
Actionable Environment Augmentation modifies the environment's feedback, transforming generic error messages into detailed, corrective hints to guide the agent during exploration.
A Fine-Grained Progress Reward provides dense, turn-by-turn learning signals, calculated as the average success rate across all turns, to address the sparse reward problem in long-horizon tasks.
paper_summary.summary: The research introduces ENVIRONMENT TUNING, a training paradigm that enhances large language model (LLM) agents for complex, multi-turn tool-use tasks by tuning the learning environment rather than solely the agent. It significantly improves agent performance on in-distribution benchmarks (e.g., boosting Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct from 7.00% to 36.92% on BFCL V3) and demonstrates robust generalization to out-of-distribution tasks, outperforming supervised fine-tuning and direct reinforcement learning approaches.

title: Don't Just Fine-tune the Agent, Tune the Environment

abstract: Recently, the frontier of Large Language Model (LLM) capabilities has shifted from single-turn code generation to agentic software engineering-a paradigm where models autonomously navigate, edit, and test complex repositories. While post-training methods have become the de facto approach for code agents, **agentic mid-training**-mid-training (MT) on large-scale data that mirrors authentic agentic workflows-remains critically underexplored due to substantial resource requirements, despite offering a more scalable path to instilling foundational agentic behaviors than relying solely on expensive reinforcement learning. A central challenge in realizing effective agentic mid-training is the distribution mismatch between static training data and the dynamic, feedback-rich environment of real development. To address this, we present a systematic study of agentic mid-training, establishing both the data synthesis principles and training methodology for effective agent development at scale. Central to our approach is **agent-native data**-supervision comprising two complementary types of trajectories: **contextually-native trajectories** that preserve the complete information flow an agent experiences, offering broad coverage and diversity; and **environmentally-native trajectories** collected from executable repositories where observations stem from actual tool invocations and test executions, providing depth and interaction authenticity. We verify the model's agentic capabilities on `SWE-Bench Verified`. We demonstrate our superiority over the previous open software engineering mid-training recipe `Kimi-Dev` under two post-training settings with an aligned base model and agentic scaffold, while using less than half mid-training tokens (73.1B). Besides relative advantage, our best performing 32B and 72B models achieve **56.1%** and **58.5%** resolution rates, respectively, which are ...

keyInsights: Mid-training with agent-native data significantly boosts agentic capabilities, providing a more robust and efficient pathway than relying solely on post-training, even for models starting from non-coder base models.
The "agent-native data" paradigm effectively addresses the distribution mismatch, enabling models to internalize the complete information flow and environmental dynamics of software development.

Agentic mid-training not only enhances performance on specific software engineering tasks but also improves general code generation and scientific reasoning abilities, suggesting a broader development of cognitive skills.
originalProblem: Existing post-training methods for code agents are limited by scarce, high-quality agentic trajectories and are constrained by the base model's intrinsic capacities for complex multi-step reasoning.

Prior mid-training efforts for code models utilize "factorized" data, which fails to capture the dynamic action-observation loop and procedural context inherent in real software development, leading to a distribution mismatch.

Effective code agents require models to coordinate diverse skills (e.g., navigation, editing, testing) within a coherent, iterative problem-solving loop, a capability that static training data largely neglects.
results: daVinci-Dev-72B achieved a 58.5% resolution rate on SWE-Bench Verified, establishing the highest performance among open training recipes using agentic scaffolds, with daVinci-Dev-32B reaching 56.1%.

The method demonstrated superior token efficiency, surpassing the previous open state-of-the-art (KIMI-DEV) using significantly less mid-training data (73.1B vs. ~150B tokens), indicating high information density.

Models trained with agent-native mid-training showed substantial generalization, improving HumanEval by +12.46% (72B) and +23.26% (32B), EvalPlus by +13.41% (72B) and +21.18% (32B), and gains on scientific reasoning benchmarks.
solution: Introduced "agent-native mid-training," an intermediate training stage designed to instill foundational agentic behaviors in LLMs at scale, before subsequent supervised fine-tuning.

Developed two types of agent-native data: "contextually-native" trajectories (D_ctx) from GitHub Pull Requests to preserve full problem-solving context, and "environmentally-native" trajectories (D_env) derived from agent rollouts in real executable environments to capture iterative feedback.
Trained daVinci-Dev models (32B and 72B) using Qwen2.5-Base with a sequential mid-training process involving D_ctx and D_env, followed by optional Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at SII, SJTU, and GAIR developed daVinci-Dev, a framework that employs agent-native mid-training to instill foundational agentic behaviors in large language models for software engineering. This approach, leveraging contextually-native and environmentally-native data, achieves a 58.5% resolution rate on SWE-Bench Verified for the 72B model, setting a new open benchmark while demonstrating token efficiency and generalization across various coding and reasoning tasks.

title: daVinci-Dev: Agent-native Mid-training for Software Engineering

abstract: Training capable software engineering (SWE) agents demands large-scale, executable, and verifiable environments that provide dynamic feedback loops for iterative code editing, test execution, and solution refinement. However, existing open-source datasets remain limited in scale and repository diversity, while industrial solutions are opaque with unreleased infrastructure, creating a prohibitive barrier for most academic research groups. We present OpenSWE, the largest fully transparent framework for SWE agent training in Python, comprising 45,320 executable Docker environments spanning over 12.8k repositories, with all Dockerfiles, evaluation scripts, and infrastructure fully open-sourced for reproducibility. OpenSWE is built through a multi-agent synthesis pipeline deployed across a 64-node distributed cluster, automating repository exploration, Dockerfile construction, evaluation script generation, and iterative test analysis. Beyond scale, we propose a quality-centric filtering pipeline that characterizes the inherent difficulty of each environment, filtering out instances that are either unsolvable or insufficiently challenging and retaining only those that maximize learning efficiency. With $891K spent on environment construction and an additional $576K on trajectory sampling and difficulty-aware curation, the entire project represents a total investment of approximately $1.47 million, yielding about 13,000 curated trajectories from roughly 9,000 quality guaranteed environments. Extensive experiments validate OpenSWE's effectiveness: OpenSWE-32B and OpenSWE-72B achieve 62.4% and 66.0% on SWE-bench Verified, establishing SOTA among Qwen2.5 series. Moreover, SWE-focused training yields substantial out-of-domain improvements, including up to 12 points on mathematical reasoning and 5 points on science benchmarks, without degrading factual recall.

keyInsights: High-quality, large-scale, and transparent executable environments are crucial for advancing autonomous SWE agent capabilities, enabling iterative refinement through dynamic feedback loops.
Data quality, particularly through difficulty-aware curation, is a critical complement to raw data scale, leading to more efficient and effective learning for SWE agents.

SWE-focused training on curated environments can generalize problem-solving skills to out-of-domain tasks, such as mathematical and scientific reasoning, without degrading pre-training-dependent factual recall.
originalProblem: Autonomous software engineering (SWE) agents powered by Large Language Models require verifiable, executable environments for dynamic feedback, but existing open-source datasets are limited in scale and diversity.
Generating and validating these environments incurs substantial computational and infrastructure costs, creating a resource barrier for many academic research groups.

Environments synthesized from real repositories often suffer from quality issues, such as PR-Issue misalignment where a patch does not resolve the stated issue, or triviality, which provides little learning signal.
results: OpenSWE provides 45,320 executable Docker environments derived from over 12.8k repositories, representing the largest fully transparent framework of its kind, with a total project investment approaching $1.47 million.

Models trained on OpenSWE achieved resolution rates of 62.4% (OpenSWE-32B) and 66.0% (OpenSWE-72B) on SWE-Bench Verified, with a combined training (mixing SWE-rebench) leading to 68.0% for 72B models.

Performance on SWE-Bench Verified improved approximately log-linearly with increased training steps, with 72B models consistently outperforming 32B counterparts and showing no saturation at the largest training step counts.
solution: Constructed OpenSWE, a large-scale, fully transparent framework using a multi-agent synthesis pipeline deployed across a 64-node distributed cluster to create executable Docker environments.

Implemented a multi-agent system (Repository Exploration, Dockerfile Construction, Evaluation Script Construction, Test Analysis agents) to transform GitHub Pull Requests into reproducible Docker containers with validated evaluation scripts.

Developed a quality-centric filtering and curation process, involving large-scale trajectory sampling, to identify and retain approximately 9,000 high-quality environments and 13,000 curated trajectories, hypothesized to maximize learning efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the Shenzhen Institute of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics for Society, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, and General Artificial Intelligence Research developed OpenSWE, a large-scale, fully transparent framework that synthesizes over 45,000 executable Docker environments for training software engineering (SWE) agents. Models trained on OpenSWE achieve up to 68.0% resolution rate on SWE-Bench Verified and demonstrate improvements in general reasoning tasks without degrading factual recall.

title: daVinci-Env: Open SWE Environment Synthesis at Scale

abstract:
We introduce SkyRL-Agent, a framework for efficient, multi-turn, long-horizon agent training and evaluation. It provides efficient asynchronous dispatching, lightweight tool integration, and flexible backend interoperability, enabling seamless use with existing RL frameworks such as SkyRL-train, VeRL, and Tinker.
Using SkyRL-Agent, we train SA-SWE-32B, a software engineering agent trained from Qwen3-32B (24.4% Pass@1) purely with reinforcement learning. We introduce two key components: an optimized asynchronous pipeline dispatcher that achieves a 1.55x speedup over naive asynchronous batching, and a tool-enhanced training recipe leveraging an AST-based search tool to facilitate code navigation, boost rollout Pass@K, and improve training efficiency. Together, these optimizations enable SA-SWE-32B to reach 39.4% Pass@1 on SWE-Bench Verified with more than 2x cost reduction compared to prior models reaching similar performance. Despite being trained solely on SWE tasks, SA-SWE-32B generalizes effectively to other agentic tasks, including Terminal-Bench, BrowseComp-Plus, and WebArena. We further demonstrate SkyRL-Agent's extensibility through case studies on deep research, computer use, and memory agents, each trained using a different training backend.

keyInsights: Efficient rollout scheduling with the Async Pipeline dispatcher significantly improves GPU utilization (around 90%) and overall training speed for multi-turn RL.

A transition-based data format (`input_tokens, output_tokens, log_probabilities`) ensures token-level fidelity and algorithmic flexibility, mitigating inference-training engine mismatches and supporting diverse RL algorithms.

Enhanced tools, such as an AST-based search tool, and structured hints for agent recovery are crucial for overcoming sparse rewards and stabilizing training in long-horizon, multi-turn tasks like software engineering.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent training often relies on ad-hoc solutions for multi-turn interactions, leading to inefficient, brittle execution, and challenging debugging for complex RL setups.

Current frameworks lack a modular, performant orchestration layer for generating, scheduling, and evaluating multi-turn agent trajectories, especially regarding asynchronous rollouts and diverse tool integrations.

Inefficient scheduling of heterogeneous operations (CPU-bound initialization/reward, GPU-bound inference) during multi-turn RL rollouts results in underutilization of hardware resources, particularly GPUs.
results: SA-SWE-32B, trained with SKYRL-AGENT, achieved 39.4% Pass@1 on SWE-Bench Verified, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art open-recipe models while incurring over 2x lower training cost (4601 H100 hours vs. 9180 H100 hours for DeepSWE).
The asynchronous pipeline dispatcher delivered a 1.55x speedup over naive asynchronous batching during training, maintaining high GPU utilization.

SA-SWE-32B demonstrated generalization to out-of-domain agentic benchmarks, outperforming baselines on Terminal-Bench (16.25% vs 13.75% accuracy) and showing improved tool-use on BrowseComp-Plus and WebArena.
solution: A tool-centric agent loop is introduced, abstracting all agent interactions and state modifications (including context management) as OpenAI-style function calls to simplify tool and task integration.
A fine-grained asynchronous dispatcher employs an 'Async Pipeline' strategy to overlap CPU-bound operations with GPU-bound LLM inference, maximizing heterogeneous hardware utilization.

A backend bridge facilitates training-backend agnosticism by using a transition-based data representation, allowing agents to run unmodified across various RL frameworks like SkyRL-train, VeRL, and Tinker.
paper_summary.summary: SKYRL-AGENT, developed by NovaSky AI, UC Berkeley, and Anyscale, is a framework designed for efficient multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) training of large language model (LLM) agents, addressing bottlenecks in rollout orchestration and resource utilization. It demonstrated a 1.55x speedup in agent rollout and reduced training costs by over 2x for its SA-SWE-32B model, which achieved 39.4% Pass@1 on SWE-Bench Verified.

title: SkyRL-Agent: Efficient RL Training for Multi-turn LLM Agent

abstract:
The emergence of large language model (LLM)-based agents has significantly
advanced the development of autonomous machine learning (ML) engineering.
However, most existing approaches rely heavily on manual prompt engineering,
failing to adapt and optimize based on diverse experimental experiences.
Focusing on this, for the first time, we explore the paradigm of learning-based
agentic ML, where an LLM agent learns through interactive experimentation on ML
tasks using online reinforcement learning (RL). To realize this, we propose a
novel agentic ML training framework with three key components: (1)
exploration-enriched fine-tuning, which enables LLM agents to generate diverse
actions for enhanced RL exploration; (2) step-wise RL, which enables training
on a single action step, accelerating experience collection and improving
training efficiency; (3) an agentic ML-specific reward module, which unifies
varied ML feedback signals into consistent rewards for RL optimization.
Leveraging this framework, we train ML-Agent, driven by a 7B-sized Qwen-2.5 LLM
for autonomous ML. Remarkably, despite being trained on merely 9 ML tasks, our
7B-sized ML-Agent outperforms the 671B-sized DeepSeek-R1 agent. Furthermore, it
achieves continuous performance improvements and demonstrates exceptional
cross-task generalization capabilities.

keyInsights: Learning-based agentic ML represents a fundamental paradigm shift from "design-time" prompt optimization to "run-time" policy learning, enabling continuous improvement through environmental interaction rather than manual refinement

Step-wise RL training dramatically improves efficiency by avoiding costly multi-step online rollouts during training, making reinforcement learning feasible for time-consuming ML experiments that can take hours to complete

Task diversity during RL training drives generalization to unseen ML tasks rather than just refinement on familiar ones, with performance on held-out tasks improving monotonically as training task count increases from 0 to 9
originalProblem: Current LLM-based autonomous ML agents rely heavily on manual prompt engineering, preventing them from adapting strategies based on experimental experience or generalizing across different task-solving trajectories

Applying online reinforcement learning to ML engineering faces three major challenges: limited exploration where agents propose similar actions, slow experience collection due to time-consuming ML experiments, and complex reward design for varied outcomes including errors and performance metrics
Existing AutoML frameworks are constrained by fixed configurations and predefined search spaces, lacking the adaptability to learn from real-world experimental feedback and evolve their approaches
results: ML-Agent consistently outperforms five strong baselines including much larger models (671B DeepSeek-R1, GPT-4o) across 3 held-in and 10 held-out tasks spanning image, text, and tabular data modalities

Achieves superior performance compared to AIDE (current SOTA tree-search-based autonomous ML agent) on nearly all 13 evaluated tasks, demonstrating strong cross-task generalization despite training on only 9 ML tasks

Ablation studies confirm exploration-enriched fine-tuning increases action diversity and prevents generalization failures, step-wise RL achieves faster convergence than episode-wise approaches, and the reward module's format constraint handling prevents the largest performance degradations
solution: Introduce exploration-enriched fine-tuning that generates diverse action pools from fast-executable ML tasks, selects the most diverse ideas using embedding distances, and uses these to prompt expert agents for broader strategy collection

Develop step-wise RL paradigm that reformulates the standard RL objective to optimize expected reward at each individual step rather than full trajectories, decoupling state sampling from real-time policy rollouts using pre-collected expert distributions

Design agentic ML-specific reward module that converts diverse feedback signals into unified scalar rewards: 0 for invalid actions/errors, 0.5 for valid non-editing actions, and sigmoid-scaled performance improvements for successful code edits
paper_summary.summary: Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Shanghai AI Laboratory researchers develop ML-Agent, the first reinforcement learning framework for autonomous machine learning engineering that enables LLM agents to learn and adapt their strategies through interactive experimentation rather than relying on static prompt engineering, with their 7B parameter model outperforming baselines including the 671B DeepSeek-R1 across 13 ML tasks by combining exploration-enriched fine-tuning, step-wise RL training that reformulates multi-step rollouts into efficient single-step updates, and an agentic ML-specific reward module that handles diverse execution outcomes from performance improvements to compilation errors.

title: ML-Agent: Reinforcing LLM Agents for Autonomous Machine Learning  Engineering

abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled software engineering agents to tackle complex code modification tasks. Most existing approaches rely on execution feedback from containerized environments, which require dependency-complete setup and physical execution of programs and tests. While effective, this paradigm is resource-intensive and difficult to maintain, substantially complicating agent training and limiting scalability. We propose SWE-World, a Docker-free framework that replaces physical execution environments with a learned surrogate for training and evaluating software engineering agents. SWE-World leverages LLM-based models trained on real agent-environment interaction data to predict intermediate execution outcomes and final test feedback, enabling agents to learn without interacting with physical containerized environments. This design preserves the standard agent-environment interaction loop while eliminating the need for costly environment construction and maintenance during agent optimization and evaluation. Furthermore, because SWE-World can simulate the final evaluation outcomes of candidate trajectories without real submission, it enables selecting the best solution among multiple test-time attempts, thereby facilitating effective test-time scaling (TTS) in software engineering tasks. Experiments on SWE-bench Verified demonstrate that SWE-World raises Qwen2.5-Coder-32B from 6.2\% to 52.0\% via Docker-free SFT, 55.0\% with Docker-free RL, and 68.2\% with further TTS. The code is available at this https URL

keyInsights: LLM-based surrogate models (SWT and SWR) can accurately and reliably approximate physical execution feedback, effectively decoupling agent development from resource-heavy container infrastructure.
Chain-of-Thought reasoning is crucial for the SWR to synthesize accurate pass/fail signals from complex test reports, preventing reward hacking and ensuring stable reinforcement learning.
The Docker-free nature of SWE-World unlocks vast amounts of previously inaccessible real-world GitHub data, overcoming limitations imposed by brittle Docker build environments.
originalProblem: Large Language Model-powered software engineering agents heavily rely on resource-intensive, Docker-based physical execution environments for feedback, creating scalability bottlenecks.
This Docker-centric paradigm limits data utilization, restricts efficient large-scale training (especially for reinforcement learning), and makes test-time exploration computationally expensive.
Complex dependency management and environment setup associated with Docker hinder the widespread adoption and research accessibility of advanced software engineering agents.
results: SWE-World achieves a resolve rate of 68.2% on SWE-bench Verified with a Qwen2.5-Coder-32B agent using SWR-based Test-Time Scaling (TTS@8), outperforming prior best results.

Docker-free Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) within SWE-World enable agent performance comparable to or exceeding Docker-trained baselines (e.g., 55.0% resolve rate for Qwen2.5-Coder-32B with RL).
The SWT and SWR models demonstrate high fidelity, with SWR-72B achieving 0.770 Accuracy and 0.780 Precision in predicting test outcomes compared to Docker ground truth.
solution: The paper introduces SWE-World, a novel Docker-free framework that entirely replaces physical execution environments with LLM-based surrogate models for agent training and evaluation.
A SWE-World Transition Model (SWT) predicts step-level execution feedback (stdout, stderr, exit code), and a SWE-World Reward Model (SWR) generates structured test reports and binary rewards.
A lightweight sandbox handles file system operations, while SWT and SWR simulate complex command execution and test verification, enabling an end-to-end Docker-free pipeline.
paper_summary.summary: The SWE-World framework replaces Docker-based physical execution environments with learned Large Language Model surrogates for training and evaluating software engineering agents. This approach achieves a 68.2% resolve rate on SWE-bench Verified, enabling Docker-free and highly scalable agent development while unlocking previously inaccessible real-world code data.

title: SWE-World: Building Software Engineering Agents in Docker-Free Environments

abstract: Multi-turn LLM agents are increasingly important for solving complex, interactive tasks, and reinforcement learning (RL) is a key ingredient for improving their long-horizon behavior. However, RL training requires generating large numbers of sandboxed rollout trajectories, and existing infrastructures often couple rollout orchestration with the training loop, making systems hard to migrate and maintain. Under the rollout-as-a-service philosophy, we present ProRL Agent , a scalable infrastructure that serves the full agentic rollout lifecycle through an API service. ProRL Agent also provides standardized and extensible sandbox environments that support diverse agentic tasks in rootless HPC settings. We validate ProRL Agent through RL training on software engineering, math, STEM, and coding tasks. ProRL Agent is open-sourced and integrated as part of NVIDIA NeMo Gym.

keyInsights: Decoupling the I/O-intensive rollout process from the GPU-intensive policy training significantly improves resource utilization, throughput, and system maintainability for multi-turn LLM agent RL.

HPC-compatible containerization (Singularity) combined with optimized, low-latency tool backends is crucial for enabling efficient, secure, and scalable agent rollouts in shared computing environments.

Maintaining token-in/token-out communication throughout the pipeline and implementing asynchronous, phase-aware job scheduling are essential for ensuring stable RL training and maximizing hardware utilization, particularly for data-efficient algorithms like DAPO.
originalProblem: Training multi-turn LLM agents with reinforcement learning (RL) requires generating numerous sandboxed rollout trajectories, which is bottlenecked by heterogeneous environments and non-instantaneous feedback.

Existing agentic RL frameworks tightly couple I/O-intensive rollout orchestration with GPU-intensive RL policy training, leading to resource conflicts, reduced efficiency, and difficulties in system migration and maintenance.
Current agentic sandbox environments often rely on Docker, which requires root-equivalent privileges, making them incompatible with many shared High-Performance Computing (HPC) clusters.
results: ProRL Agent achieved performance gains on SWE-Bench Verified, improving Qwen3-8B from a 9.6 baseline to 18.0 (nearly 2x) and Qwen3-14B from 15.4 to 23.6, outperforming prior frameworks like SkyRL-Agent.

The framework demonstrated generality across diverse agent domains, showing steady RL training improvements for STEM agents (mean reward 0.2 to 0.65), Math agents (Pass@1 0.4 to 0.9 on AMC), and Code agents (Pass@1 0.23 to 0.42 on Codeforces).

System analysis confirmed near-linear scalability of rollout throughput with increasing compute nodes and showed that components like Stale Job Cleanup, Efficient Bash, and Load Balancing individually contributed to higher GPU utilization and reduced action execution times.
solution: ProRL Agent implements a "rollout-as-a-service" architecture that decouples the agentic rollout lifecycle from the RL trainer via an HTTP API, allowing independent optimization and deployment.

It utilizes Singularity for rootless, HPC-compatible containerized sandbox environments, featuring pluggable task abstraction and optimized tool backends like `ptyprocess` for Bash and direct IPython kernel connections to minimize latency.

The system employs an asynchronous three-stage pipeline for rollout orchestration, dynamic LLM backend management with load balancing, and token-in/token-out communication to prevent re-tokenization drift and ensure training stability.
paper_summary.summary: ProRL Agent, developed at NVIDIA, introduces a "rollout-as-a-service" infrastructure for training multi-turn LLM agents, decoupling I/O-intensive rollout generation from GPU-intensive policy training. This framework demonstrated consistent performance gains, improving the Qwen3-8B model's performance on SWE-Bench Verified from 9.6 to 18.0 and the Qwen3-14B model from 15.4 to 23.6, while exhibiting generality across STEM, Math, and Code agent domains.

title: ProRL Agent: Rollout-as-a-Service for RL Training of Multi-Turn LLM Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities when trained within executable runtime environments, notably excelling at software engineering tasks through verified feedback loops. Yet, scalable and generalizable execution-grounded environments remain scarce, limiting progress in training more capable ML agents. We introduce CTF-Dojo, the first large-scale executable runtime tailored for training LLMs with verifiable feedback, featuring 658 fully functional Capture-The-Flag (CTF)-style challenges containerized in Docker with guaranteed reproducibility. To enable rapid scaling without manual intervention, we develop CTF-Forge, an automated pipeline that transforms publicly available artifacts into ready-to-use execution environments in minutes, eliminating weeks of expert configuration traditionally required. We trained LLM-based agents on just 486 high-quality, execution-verified trajectories from CTF-Dojo, achieving up to 11.6% absolute gains over strong baselines across three competitive benchmarks: InterCode-CTF, NYU CTF Bench, and Cybench. Our best-performing 32B model reaches 31.9% Pass@1, establishing a new open-weight state-of-the-art that rivals frontier models like DeepSeek-V3-0324 and Gemini-2.5-Flash. By framing CTF-style tasks as a benchmark for executable-agent learning, CTF-Dojo demonstrates that execution-grounded training signals are not only effective but pivotal in advancing high-performance ML agents without dependence on costly proprietary systems.
title: Training Language Model Agents to Find Vulnerabilities with CTF-Dojo

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as tool-augmented agents for multi-step decision making, yet training robust tool-using agents remains challenging. Existing methods still require manual intervention, depend on non-verifiable simulated environments, rely exclusively on either supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning (RL), and struggle with stable long-horizon, multi-turn learning. To address these challenges, we introduce ASTRA, a fully automated end-to-end framework for training tool-augmented language model agents via scalable data synthesis and verifiable reinforcement learning. ASTRA integrates two complementary components. First, a pipeline that leverages the static topology of tool-call graphs synthesizes diverse, structurally grounded trajectories, instilling broad and transferable tool-use competence. Second, an environment synthesis framework that captures the rich, compositional topology of human semantic reasoning converts decomposed question-answer traces into independent, code-executable, and rule-verifiable environments, enabling deterministic multi-turn RL. Based on this method, we develop a unified training methodology that integrates SFT with online RL using trajectory-level rewards to balance task completion and interaction efficiency. Experiments on multiple agentic tool-use benchmarks demonstrate that ASTRA-trained models achieve state-of-the-art performance at comparable scales, approaching closed-source systems while preserving core reasoning ability. We release the full pipelines, environments, and trained models at this https URL.

keyInsights: Online reinforcement learning in verifiable environments provides the largest performance boost for agentic capabilities, particularly for long-horizon planning.
Strategically mixing irrelevant tools during RL training is crucial for developing robust tool discrimination and preventing policy overfitting.
An F1-style trajectory-level reward, combining recall and precision, is essential for stable training, balancing task completion with interaction efficiency.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent training methods over-rely on manual intervention for synthesizing tool-use data and constructing training environments, limiting scalability.
Current reinforcement learning setups often use LLM-simulated environments that lack verifiability, leading to unstable long-horizon, multi-turn online RL.
Many approaches decompose multi-turn trajectories into single-step instances, hindering the agent's ability to learn coherent, long-horizon decision-making.
results: ASTRA-trained models (e.g., Astra-32B-thinking-v1) achieved state-of-the-art performance on agentic tool-use benchmarks (e.g., BFCL-MT, τ²-Bench) among open-source models, outperforming baselines and competing with some closed-source systems.
The training methodology preserves core reasoning ability, showing negligible degradation on non-agentic complex reasoning benchmarks compared to base models.
Ablation studies confirmed the F1-style reward's role in stable training and the critical impact of irrelevant tool mixing for effective tool discrimination.
solution: A multi-turn trajectory synthesis pipeline leverages tool-call graph topology and automated quality assessment for robust Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) data generation.
Automated synthesis of independent, rule-verifiable, and code-executable environments enables deterministic multi-turn online Reinforcement Learning (RL).
A unified training methodology integrates SFT to build a strong initial policy and refines it through online RL with an F1-style reward and strategic irrelevant tool mixing.
paper_summary.summary: ASTRA introduces a fully automated framework for training tool-augmented language model agents, synthesizing multi-turn trajectories for supervised fine-tuning and creating verifiable, code-executable environments for reinforcement learning. This approach achieved state-of-the-art performance on agentic tool-use benchmarks while preserving core reasoning abilities.

title: ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas

abstract: Existing agents for solving tasks such as ML engineering rely on prompting powerful language models. As a result, these agents do not improve with more experience. In this paper, we show that agents backed by weaker models that improve via reinforcement learning (RL) can outperform agents backed by much larger, but static models. We identify two major challenges with RL in this setting. First, actions can take a variable amount of time (e.g., executing code for different solutions), which leads to asynchronous policy gradient updates that favor faster but suboptimal solutions. To tackle variable-duration actions, we propose duration- aware gradient updates in a distributed asynchronous RL framework to amplify high-cost but high-reward actions. Second, using only test split performance as a reward provides limited feedback. A program that is nearly correct is treated the same as one that fails entirely. To address this, we propose environment instrumentation to offer partial credit, distinguishing almost-correct programs from those that fail early (e.g., during data loading). Environment instrumentation uses a separate static language model to insert print statement to an existing program to log the agent's experimental progress, from which partial credit can be extracted as reward signals for learning. Our experimental results on MLEBench suggest that performing gradient updates on a much smaller model (Qwen2.5-3B) trained with RL outperforms prompting a much larger model (Claude-3.5-Sonnet) with agent scaffolds, by an average of 22% across 12 Kaggle tasks.

keyInsights: Reinforcement learning, augmented with specific mechanisms, allows smaller, open-weight language models to surpass the performance of larger, static, prompted frontier models in complex agentic tasks.
Weighting gradient updates by action duration is crucial for effective RL in environments with variable-time actions, preventing the agent from converging to fast but suboptimal strategies.

Environment instrumentation provides a generalizable method for dense reward shaping in sparse-reward code-generation environments, guiding agents through multi-step processes more effectively than relying solely on end-task metrics.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agents, relying on prompting static models, do not fundamentally improve from continuous experience, leading to performance plateaus in complex tasks like machine learning engineering (MLE).
Applying reinforcement learning to agentic tasks is challenging due to actions having highly variable execution times, which biases asynchronous RL towards faster, potentially suboptimal solutions.
Sparse reward signals, such as a single test set performance score, for multi-step MLE tasks hinder effective learning by failing to provide granular feedback for intermediate steps.
results: The RL-trained Qwen2.5-3B model outperformed frontier models like Claude-3.5-Sonnet and GPT-4o by an average of 22% and 24% respectively, achieving the best performance on 8 out of 12 MLEBench tasks.
Ablation studies confirmed that duration-aware gradients prevented the agent from converging to faster, suboptimal solutions, enabling the exploration of higher-reward, longer-duration actions.

Environment instrumentation for partial credit accelerated learning and improved the consistency of task scores, while explicit self-improvement prompting yielded an average 8% performance boost across tasks.
solution: Introduces duration-aware gradient updates that weight each gradient contribution by the action's execution duration, counteracting the bias towards faster actions in asynchronous RL.

Implements environment instrumentation using a separate static LLM to insert verifiable print statements into agent-generated code, providing dense partial credit rewards for successfully completed intermediate milestones.
Trains the agent with a multi-step RL process that includes an explicit self-improvement prompt, encouraging it to iterate and refine its own solutions based on execution feedback.
paper_summary.summary: A study from Stanford University introduces a reinforcement learning framework that enables smaller language models to function as self-improving machine learning engineering agents, achieving up to 24% better performance than prompting larger, static frontier models like GPT-4o. This is accomplished through novel duration-aware gradient updates and environment instrumentation for dense partial credit signals.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Machine Learning Engineering Agents

abstract: While large language model agents have advanced software engineering tasks, the unscalable nature of existing test-based supervision is limiting the potential improvement of data scaling. The reason is twofold: (1) building and running test sandbox is rather heavy and fragile, and (2) data with high-coverage tests is naturally rare and threatened by test hacking via edge cases. In this paper, we propose R4P, a patch verifier model to provide scalable rewards for training and testing SWE agents via reasoning. We consider that patch verification is fundamentally a reasoning task, mirroring how human repository maintainers review patches without writing and running new reproduction tests. To obtain sufficient reference and reduce the risk of reward hacking, R4P uses a group-wise objective for RL training, enabling it to verify multiple patches against each other's modification and gain a dense reward for stable training. R4P achieves 72.2% Acc. for verifying patches from SWE-bench-verified, surpassing OpenAI o3. To demonstrate R4P's practicality, we design and train a lite scaffold, Mini-SE, with pure reinforcement learning where all rewards are derived from R4P. As a result, Mini-SE achieves 26.2% Pass@1 on SWE-bench-verified, showing a 10.0% improvement over the original Qwen3-32B. This can be further improved to 32.8% with R4P for test-time scaling. Furthermore, R4P verifies patches within a second, 50x faster than testing on average. The stable scaling curves of rewards and accuracy along with high efficiency reflect R4P's practicality.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: Patch verification is fundamentally a reasoning task, similar to human code review, which can be effectively modeled by LLMs to overcome limitations of execution-based testing.

Evaluating a group of candidate patches rather than individual ones provides crucial comparative context, mitigates context deficiency, and generates a denser, more stable reward signal for reinforcement learning.

Decoupling patch verification from execution-based testing significantly improves efficiency and scalability, enabling the utilization of massive, previously unsupervisable datasets for training software agents.
originalProblem: Traditional test-based supervision for LLM software engineering agents relies on heavy, fragile infrastructure for test execution, limiting scalability and parallel processing.
A scarcity of high-quality, high-coverage test data restricts training, leaving vast amounts of real-world issues in open-source projects unutilized.
Existing tests often suffer from low coverage, allowing LLM agents to generate "tricky patches" that pass tests but do not genuinely fix the underlying issue.
results: R4P achieved 72.2% accuracy on the SWE-bench-verified dataset, outperforming larger proprietary models and specialized trajectory verifiers.
The lightweight Mini-SE agent, trained purely with R4P's rewards, reached a 26.2% Pass@1 resolution rate on SWE-bench-verified, a 10.0% improvement over the base model.
R4P verifies patches in approximately one second, demonstrating over a 50x speedup compared to traditional test-based verification, which averaged 50 seconds and frequently timed out.
solution: Introduction of R4P (Reasoning for Patch Verifier), a novel model that frames patch verification as a reasoning task, moving away from execution-based methods.
R4P employs a group-wise task formulation where multiple candidate patches are compared, providing richer contextual information and a denser reward signal for stable reinforcement learning.
R4P is trained with an RL algorithm on carefully sampled patch groups and used to supervise a lightweight, execution-free agent (Mini-SE) entirely without traditional sandbox testing.
paper_summary.summary: A collaborative team from The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, and ByteDance developed R4P, a reasoning-based patch verifier that provides scalable, test-free supervision for Large Language Model (LLM) agents in software engineering. This approach enables efficient training and evaluation by leveraging a group-wise task formulation, unlocking vast amounts of previously unusable, untested real-world software issues.

title: Scalable Supervising Software Agents with Patch Reasoner

abstract: Conducting reinforcement learning (RL) in simulated environments offers a cost-effective and highly scalable way to enhance language-based agents. However, previous work has been limited to semi-automated environment synthesis or tasks lacking sufficient difficulty, offering little breadth or depth. In addition, the instability of simulated users integrated into these environments, along with the heterogeneity across simulated environments, poses further challenges for agentic RL. In this work, we propose: (1) a unified pipeline for automated and scalable synthesis of simulated environments associated with high-difficulty but easily verifiable tasks; and (2) an environment level RL algorithm that not only effectively mitigates user instability but also performs advantage estimation at the environment level, thereby improving training efficiency and stability. Comprehensive evaluations on agentic benchmarks, including tau-bench, tau2-Bench, and VitaBench, validate the effectiveness of our proposed method. Further in-depth analyses underscore its out-of-domain generalization.

keyInsights: Automated synthesis of diverse and complex environments is vital for scalable and effective agentic RL, significantly reducing reliance on manual data creation.
Addressing the inherent instability of LLM-based simulated users is critical for accurate advantage estimation and robust agent training, preventing misleading learning signals.
Environment-level normalization of advantage estimates provides more stable and reliable reward signals compared to group-level approaches, improving multi-environment reinforcement learning.
originalProblem: Current environment synthesis for LLM-based agents is often manual or semi-automated, limiting scalability and the complexity of generated tasks.
LLM-based simulated users in agentic RL benchmarks frequently exhibit instability and errors, leading to misleading feedback for agents.
Traditional multi-environment RL training methods often suffer from unstable advantage estimation and suboptimal learning efficiency due to isolated or poorly normalized environment data.
results: AutoForge-30B-A3B surpassed its backbone and open-source models on benchmarks like "\u03c4-bench" and VitaBench, achieving performance competitive with several advanced closed-source LLMs.
The framework demonstrated strong out-of-domain generalization, improving agent performance on ACEBench-zh despite language differences and unseen tool formats.

In-depth analysis confirmed that more capable user agents, environment-level advantage estimation, and the MEU mechanism each contributed to significantly more stable training curves and higher agent performance.
solution: A unified, automated pipeline generates verifiable interactive environments and high-difficulty tasks solely from tool descriptions.
The Environment-level Relative Policy Optimization (ERPO) algorithm incorporates "Masking Out Erroneous User Behaviors" (MEU) using an LLM-as-judge to filter unreliable simulated user interactions.
ERPO performs advantage estimation at the environment level, normalizing returns within each environment to enhance training efficiency and stability.
paper_summary.summary: AutoForge, developed by Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group, presents a framework for automating the synthesis of challenging simulated environments and tasks for agentic reinforcement learning.
