It introduces an Environment-level Relative Policy Optimization (ERPO) algorithm to improve training stability and agent performance, achieving results comparable to advanced closed-source LLMs and demonstrating strong out-of-domain generalization.

title: AutoForge: Automated Environment Synthesis for Agentic Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has been widely adopted as the de facto method for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models and has demonstrated notable success in verifiable domains like math and competitive programming tasks. However, the efficacy of RLVR diminishes significantly when applied to agentic environments. These settings, characterized by multi-step, complex problem solving, lead to high failure rates even for frontier LLMs, as the reward landscape is too sparse for effective model training via conventional RLVR. In this work, we introduce Agent-RLVR, a framework that makes RLVR effective in challenging agentic settings, with an initial focus on software engineering tasks. Inspired by human pedagogy, Agent-RLVR introduces agent guidance, a mechanism that actively steers the agent towards successful trajectories by leveraging diverse informational cues. These cues, ranging from high-level strategic plans to dynamic feedback on the agent's errors and environmental interactions, emulate a teacher's guidance, enabling the agent to navigate difficult solution spaces and promotes active self-improvement via additional environment exploration. In the Agent-RLVR training loop, agents first attempt to solve tasks to produce initial trajectories, which are then validated by unit tests and supplemented with agent guidance. Agents then reattempt with guidance, and the agent policy is updated with RLVR based on the rewards of these guided trajectories. Agent-RLVR elevates the pass@1 performance of Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct from 9.4% to 22.4% on SWE-Bench Verified. We find that our guidance-augmented RLVR data is additionally useful for test-time reward model training, shown by further boosting pass@1 to 27.8%. Agent-RLVR lays the groundwork for training agents with RLVR in complex, real-world environments where conventional RL methods struggle.

keyInsights: Pedagogical 'agent guidance' is crucial for making RLVR viable in agentic environments by converting sparse reward landscapes into a denser, more learnable signal.
The guidance mechanism significantly increases the availability of positive trajectories for RL training, allowing LLMs to learn effectively from environmental feedback.
Data generated during the Agent-RLVR training, including guided successful trajectories, is valuable enough to train an effective external reward model for test-time patch selection.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) struggles in multi-step agentic environments due to sparse reward signals and a low probability of initial success.
Large language models (LLMs) face challenges in multi-turn interactions, contextual navigation, and effective tool use when acting as agents in complex problem-solving scenarios.
Training autonomous software engineering (SWE) agents is difficult because tasks require deep understanding, multi-file changes, and infrequent, delayed environmental feedback.
results: Agent-RLVR increased the PASS@1 performance of the Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct model on SWE-Bench Verified from a 9.4% baseline to 22.4%.
Performance was further boosted to 27.8% PASS@1 on SWE-Bench Verified when a test-time reward model, trained on Agent-RLVR data, was used to rank multiple generated patches.
Ablation studies confirmed that guidance is indispensable, leading to a higher number of successful rollouts and superior performance compared to training without guidance augmentation.
solution: Agent-RLVR introduces "agent guidance" (plan, environment feedback, and interaction hints) during training to actively steer LLMs towards successful problem-solving trajectories.
The training process involves an iterative loop where an agent attempts a task, and for failed attempts, an external LLM generates guidance for a reattempt, creating preference pairs.
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is then used on these guidance-augmented datasets to update the agent's policy, encouraging it to generate more successful actions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Scale AI developed Agent-RLVR, a framework that integrates 'agent guidance' into Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to effectively train large language models for complex software engineering tasks. This approach boosted the PASS@1 performance of a Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct model from 9.4% to 22.4% on SWE-Bench Verified.

title: Agent-RLVR: Training Software Engineering Agents via Guidance and Environment Rewards

abstract: LLM-based agents have shown promising capabilities in a growing range of software engineering (SWE) tasks. However, advancing this field faces two critical challenges. First, high-quality training data is scarce, especially data that reflects real-world SWE scenarios, where agents must interact with development environments, execute code and adapt behavior based on the outcomes of their actions. Existing datasets are either limited to one-shot code generation or comprise small, manually curated collections of interactive tasks, lacking both scale and diversity. Second, the lack of fresh interactive SWE tasks affects evaluation of rapidly improving models, as static benchmarks quickly become outdated due to contamination issues. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel, automated, and scalable pipeline to continuously extract real-world interactive SWE tasks from diverse GitHub repositories. Using this pipeline, we construct SWE-rebench, a public dataset comprising over 21,000 interactive Python-based SWE tasks, suitable for reinforcement learning of SWE agents at scale. Additionally, we use continuous supply of fresh tasks collected using SWE-rebench methodology to build a contamination-free benchmark for agentic software engineering. We compare results of various LLMs on this benchmark to results on SWE-bench Verified and show that performance of some language models might be inflated due to contamination issues.

keyInsights: Automated task collection can generate real-world, interactive software engineering data at a scale (over 21,000 tasks) previously unachievable through manual curation, suitable for agent training.

Evidence of benchmark contamination in older datasets like SWE-bench Verified is observed, as models demonstrate significantly higher scores on these compared to fresh, decontaminated SWE-rebench tasks.

Standardized evaluation on SWE-rebench reveals varying agentic behaviors and limitations across models, such as GPT-4.1's temporal performance shifts and Qwen2.5's struggles with instruction following and environment interaction.
originalProblem: Scarcity of large-scale, interactive training data for LLM-based software engineering (SWE) agents, particularly for reinforcement learning, due to complex environment setup.
Benchmark contamination in existing static evaluation datasets, where models may have been inadvertently exposed to test instances during training, leading to inflated performance scores.
Lack of standardized evaluation protocols across varying agent scaffolds and reporting practices, making fair comparisons of LLM agent capabilities challenging.
results: Release of the SWE-rebench dataset, comprising over 21,000 interactive Python SWE tasks extracted from 3,468 distinct GitHub repositories, publicly available on Hugging Face.
Establishment of a public, decontaminated SWE-rebench benchmark (294 executable tasks) and leaderboard that provides standardized, continuously updated evaluations for LLM agents.

Performance analysis demonstrating significant score divergence (e.g., DeepSeek-V3-0324 achieving 39.7% on SWE-bench Verified vs. 21.3% on SWE-rebench Mar-Apr), indicating potential contamination effects in older benchmarks.
solution: An automated, scalable four-stage pipeline for continuous collection of real-world, interactive SWE tasks from diverse GitHub repositories, utilizing TractoAI for distributed processing.
LLM-driven generation and iterative refinement of installation recipes and test commands, enabling automated configuration of diverse project environments.

Execution-based verification of task solvability and test integrity in isolated containerized environments, ensuring that generated tasks have failing tests that pass after applying the solution patch.
paper_summary.summary: The Nebius team developed SWE-rebench, an automated pipeline that generates over 21,000 interactive Python software engineering tasks and provides a decontaminated benchmark for evaluating LLM agents. This system offers a standardized, continuously updated resource to assess agent capabilities without prior data exposure.

title: SWE-rebench: An Automated Pipeline for Task Collection and Decontaminated Evaluation of Software Engineering Agents

abstract: Software engineering agents (SWE) are improving rapidly, with recent gains largely driven by reinforcement learning (RL). However, RL training is constrained by the scarcity of large-scale task collections with reproducible execution environments and reliable test suites. Although a growing number of benchmarks have emerged, datasets suitable for training remain limited in scale and diversity or often target a limited set of high-resource language ecosystems. We introduce SWE-rebench V2, a language-agnostic automated pipeline for harvesting executable real-world SWE tasks and constructing RL training environments at scale. The pipeline synthesizes repository-specific installation and test procedures via an interactive setup agent, and filters unsound instances using an ensemble of LLM judges, validated against human-verified SWE-bench annotations. Using this pipeline, we construct a dataset of 32,000+ tasks spanning 20 languages and 3,600+ repositories, with pre-built images for reproducible execution. To further scale training data, we additionally release 120,000+ tasks with installation instructions, fail-to-pass tests and rich metadata, where the problem statement is generated based on the original pull request description. We validate the collected instances through a diagnostic study that covers a subset of tasks in five programming languages across seven popular models, and provide instance-level metadata that flags common confounders such as overly restrictive tests and underspecified descriptions. We release the datasets, the collection and execution code, and associated artifacts to enable large-scale training of SWE agents across diverse languages and repositories.

keyInsights: Interactive LLM agents (e.g., mini-SWE-agent) significantly outperform non-interactive methods in autonomously synthesizing repository setup procedures, achieving a 25.8% `pass@1` success rate compared to 12.1%.

Employing a consensus of multiple LLM judges for issue clarity filtering is crucial for ensuring high precision (0.88 with consensus) in identifying well-specified tasks, even at the cost of some recall.

Comprehensive diagnostic analysis reveals systematic failure modes in tasks (e.g., test suite coupling, implicit naming, external dependencies), highlighting the importance of rich metadata for informed data curation and curriculum learning.
originalProblem: A scarcity of large-scale, diverse, and reproducible executable task environments hinders the training of advanced software engineering (SWE) agents, particularly for reinforcement learning.
Existing SWE benchmarks often lack broad language coverage (being primarily Python-centric) and struggle with scalability, robust test suites, and reliable execution reproducibility.

The high cost and complexity of automatically constructing high-quality, stable task environments across the heterogeneous build systems and dependency managers of various programming languages limit progress.
results: The project released "SWE-rebench V2," a core dataset of 32,079 containerized and executable SWE tasks spanning 3,617 repositories and 20 programming languages.
An expanded dataset of over 120,000 additional PR-derived tasks was provided, using pull request descriptions as problem statements to substantially scale the training corpus.

Tasks are enriched with instance-level diagnostic metadata based on identified failure categories (B1, B2, B3), enabling researchers to perform stratified filtering and design targeted curricula for agent training.
solution: A language-agnostic construction funnel was designed, combining interactive environment synthesis using an LLM-powered agent to infer installation/test procedures with automated oracle extraction.

The pipeline leverages multi-stage Docker builds for reproducible containerized execution, employs dual-pass execution with "fail-to-pass" test validation, and integrates LLM-based judges for stringent issue clarity filtering.

Additional training data is generated from pull requests (not directly linked to issues) by reusing setup recipes and synthesizing problem descriptions, alongside the provision of extensive instance-level diagnostic metadata.
paper_summary.summary: Nebius researchers developed an automated, language-agnostic pipeline that generates a large-scale collection of executable software engineering tasks for training AI agents. The work delivers 32,079 containerized tasks across 20 programming languages, supplemented by over 120,000 pull-request-derived tasks, each enriched with diagnostic metadata for improved training and evaluation.

title: SWE-rebench V2: Language-Agnostic SWE Task Collection at Scale

abstract: Tool-augmented large language models (LLMs), hereafter LLM agents, leverage external tools to solve diverse tasks and interface with the real world. However, current training practices largely rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) over static trajectories or reinforcement learning (RL) on narrow tasks, which generalize poorly beyond development settings and lead to brittleness with new tools and unseen workflows. Because code execution reflects many structural patterns of real-world workflows, we use coding problems as a structured substrate to build tool-use agent training environments with diverse task configurations. To this end, we introduce CodeGym, a scalable framework that synthesizes diverse, verifiable, and controllable multi-turn tool-use environments for agent RL, enabling LLM agents to explore and master various workflows actively. CodeGym converts static coding problems into interactive environments by extracting atomic functions or logic into callable tools, yielding verifiable tasks that span various tool-execution workflows. Models of varying sizes and chain-of-thought configurations trained in CodeGym exhibit consistent out-of-distribution generalizability; for example, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct achieves an absolute accuracy gain of 8.7 points on the OOD benchmark $\tau$-Bench. These results highlight CodeGym as a step toward scalable general-purpose RL environments for training tool-use behaviors that align with real-world agent workflows.
title: Generalizable End-to-End Tool-Use RL with Synthetic CodeGym

abstract: Recent studies have uncovered the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) in addressing complex sequential decision-making tasks through the provision of high-level instructions. However, LLM-based agents lack specialization in tackling specific target problems, particularly in real-time dynamic environments. Additionally, deploying an LLM-based agent in practical scenarios can be both costly and time-consuming. On the other hand, reinforcement learning (RL) approaches train agents that specialize in the target task but often suffer from low sampling efficiency and high exploration costs. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that addresses these challenges by training a smaller, specialized student RL agent using instructions from an LLM-based teacher agent. By incorporating the guidance from the teacher agent, the student agent can distill the prior knowledge of the LLM into its own model. Consequently, the student agent can be trained with significantly less data. Moreover, through further training with environment feedback, the student agent surpasses the capabilities of its teacher for completing the target task. We conducted experiments on challenging MiniGrid and Habitat environments, specifically designed for embodied AI research, to evaluate the effectiveness of our framework. The results clearly demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance compared to strong baseline methods. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: Large Language Model as a Policy Teacher for Training Reinforcement Learning Agents

abstract: Training generalist agents capable of adapting to diverse scenarios requires interactive environments for self-exploration. However, interactive environments remain critically scarce, and existing synthesis methods suffer from significant limitations regarding environmental diversity and scalability. To address these challenges, we introduce ScaleEnv, a framework that constructs fully interactive environments and verifiable tasks entirely from scratch. Specifically, ScaleEnv ensures environment reliability through procedural testing, and guarantees task completeness and solvability via tool dependency graph expansion and executable action verification. By enabling agents to learn through exploration within ScaleEnv, we demonstrate significant performance improvements on unseen, multi-turn tool-use benchmarks such as $\tau^2$-Bench and VitaBench, highlighting strong generalization capabilities. Furthermore, we investigate the relationship between increasing number of domains and model generalization performance, providing empirical evidence that scaling environmental diversity is critical for robust agent learning.

keyInsights: Environmental diversity is a critical scaling factor for agent learning, with an "Environment Scaling Curve" empirically showing monotonic improvement in zero-shot generalization with increased unique training domains.
Grounding environments in verified, executable code through rigorous procedural testing is crucial for providing reliable feedback and preventing hallucinations in training data.
Deterministic, rule-based reward evaluators offer more objective and robust learning signals compared to LLM-as-a-judge paradigms, mitigating reward hacking and reducing computational overhead.
originalProblem: The critical scarcity and quality limitations of interactive environments for training generalist Large Language Model (LLM) agents.
Existing LLM-simulated environments suffer from hallucinations and fail to maintain authentic states, leading to unreliable feedback for agent training.
Current environment synthesis methods lack scalability and diversity due to reliance on external documentation or intensive manual intervention.
results: Agents trained on ScaleEnv (Qwen3-SE models) consistently and substantially outperformed base models and other open-weight LLMs on unseen, out-of-distribution benchmarks (e.g., τ2-Bench and VitaBench).
Qwen3-SE-32B nearly doubled the performance of the base model on the challenging cross-domain subset of VitaBench, showcasing enhanced reasoning generalization.
Ablation studies confirmed that execution-based verification is vital for reliable data, and the rule-based reward mechanism demonstrated superior performance over LLM-as-a-judge paradigms.
solution: Developed ScaleEnv, a unified framework that decouples environment and task construction into Executable Graph Construction and Task Instantiation.
Implemented rigorous execution-based verification and procedural testing for tool and database schemas, ensuring functional reliability and accurate state transitions.
Employed a Dependency-Aware Topological Expansion and LLM-Gated Chain Expansion based on a Tool Dependency Graph to generate diverse, multi-turn, and verifiable tasks.
paper_summary.summary: ScaleEnv introduces a framework for synthesizing diverse, interactive, and verifiable training environments for generalist tool-use agents, constructed entirely from scratch. Agents trained on ScaleEnv environments demonstrate superior zero-shot generalization to unseen domains and interaction formats, with performance on VitaBench nearly doubling that of base models on cross-domain tasks, and establishing an "Environment Scaling Curve" that correlates environmental diversity with generalization performance.

title: ScaleEnv: Scaling Environment Synthesis from Scratch for Generalist Interactive Tool-Use Agent Training

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for improving large language models beyond supervised fine-tuning, yet sustaining performance gains at scale remains an open challenge, as data diversity and structure, rather than volume alone, become the limiting factor. We address this by introducing a scalable multi-turn synthetic data generation pipeline in which a teacher model iteratively refines problems based on in-context student performance summaries, producing structured difficulty progressions without any teacher fine-tuning. Compared to single-turn generation, this multi-turn approach substantially improves the yield of valid synthetic problems and naturally produces stepping stones, i.e. easier and harder variants of the same core task, that support curriculum-based training. We systematically study how task difficulty, curriculum scheduling, and environment diversity interact during RL training across the Llama3.1-8B Instruct and Qwen3-8B Base model families, with additional scaling experiments on Qwen2.5-32B. Our results show that synthetic augmentation consistently improves in-domain code and in most cases out-of-domain math performance, and we provide empirical insights into how curriculum design and data diversity jointly shape RL training dynamics.

keyInsights: Synthetic data augmentation is a crucial axis for scaling RL in code generation, consistently improving both in-domain and out-of-domain performance more effectively than just increasing real data volume.

Training exclusively on medium-difficulty problems provides the best balance between convergence speed and generalization, while reverse curricula effectively mitigate early overfitting to easy tasks and promote broader exploration.
Environment diversity acts as an independent scaling axis; distributing training across multiple distinct RL environments significantly enhances out-of-domain generalization and reduces overfitting.
originalProblem: Scaling reinforcement learning (RL) performance for code generation in large language models (LLMs) is challenging, as simply increasing data volume does not consistently improve performance.

Data diversity and structure limit RL gains, with models facing hard exploration challenges on difficult problems and inefficient resource allocation on overly easy tasks, potentially leading to entropy collapse.
Traditional curriculum sampling on real data lacks meaningful difficulty progressions, hindering effective progressive learning.
results: Synthetic data augmentation consistently improved in-domain code performance (LCB Pass@1 and Pass@10) and out-of-domain math performance (Math500, AIME2024 Pass@1 and Pass@5) across different LLMs.
Multi-turn data generation quadrupled the proportion of valid synthetic problems compared to single-turn methods and naturally produced 'stepping stones' of varying difficulty.

Reverse curricula, particularly starting from medium-level questions, demonstrated more stable learning and maintained stronger performance on harder evaluation splits, effectively mitigating overfitting to easy problems.
solution: A scalable multi-turn synthetic data generation pipeline was developed to iteratively refine problems based on student performance, increasing valid problem yield and creating 'stepping stones' of varying difficulty.
Different curriculum schedules, including a novel 'reverse curriculum' approach, were investigated to optimize training dynamics and counteract entropy collapse.

The impact of distributing computational budgets across multiple, distinct RL environments (Induction, Abduction, Deduction, Fuzzing) was systematically studied to understand how environment diversity affects generalization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Meta FAIR, University of Tübingen, and the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems developed a multi-turn synthetic data generation pipeline and explored curriculum learning strategies to enhance reinforcement learning for code generation in large language models. Their methods consistently improved in-domain code performance and out-of-domain generalization by augmenting training data with diverse synthetic problems and optimizing task difficulty progression.

title: A Deep Dive into Scaling RL for Code Generation with Synthetic Data and Curricula

abstract: With the advent of AI agents, automatic scientific discovery has become a tenable goal. Many recent works scaffold agentic systems that can perform machine learning research, but don't offer a principled way to train such agents -- and current LLMs often generate plausible-looking but ineffective ideas. To make progress on training agents that can learn from doing, we provide a novel synthetic environment generation pipeline targeting machine learning agents. Our pipeline automatically synthesizes machine learning challenges compatible with the SWE-agent framework, covering topic sampling, dataset proposal, and code generation. The resulting synthetic tasks are 1) grounded in real machine learning datasets, because the proposed datasets are verified against the Huggingface API and are 2) verified for higher quality with a self-debugging loop. To validate the effectiveness of our synthetic tasks, we tackle MLGym, a benchmark for machine learning tasks. From the synthetic tasks, we sample trajectories from a teacher model (GPT-5), then use the trajectories to train a student model (Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B). The student models trained with our synthetic tasks achieve improved performance on MLGym, raising the AUP metric by 9% for Qwen3-4B and 12% for Qwen3-8B.

keyInsights: Training AI agents on rich, long-horizon agentic trajectories from synthetic environments effectively bridges the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical execution in machine learning research.
An automated, self-debugging pipeline can successfully generate a large number of runnable and valid machine learning tasks without human intervention, ensuring task diversity and quality.

Supervised fine-tuning using detailed interaction logs, rather than just final outcomes, significantly enhances LLMs' capabilities in iterative problem-solving, debugging, and implementation refinement for complex ML tasks.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) possess extensive theoretical machine learning knowledge but lack practical, multi-step execution experience crucial for scientific discovery, such as debugging and handling experimental failures.
Existing research agents struggle because they are primarily trained on static outputs (papers, code) rather than the iterative, dynamic processes inherent in scientific research.
Manually creating diverse and challenging machine learning tasks and corresponding interaction data for agent training is resource-intensive and does not scale effectively.
results: The pipeline successfully generated and validated approximately 500 distinct synthetic ML tasks, grounded in real-world HuggingFace datasets and covering various ML sub-domains.
A dataset of 23,204 high-quality agent trajectories was collected and filtered, characterized by a mean token length of 22,074 and an average of 24.8 turns per trajectory.

Fine-tuned student models (SFT-Qwen3-4B and SFT-Qwen3-8B) achieved 9% and 12% increases, respectively, in the Area Under the Performance (AUP) metric on the MLGym benchmark, demonstrating improved performance across a majority of individual tasks.
solution: A multi-stage, automated pipeline is introduced to synthesize a large volume of diverse machine learning tasks, including topic sampling, dataset proposal from HuggingFace, and configuration/starter code generation.
An unsupervised environment verification phase employs a self-debugging loop, leveraging a GPT-5 teacher model, to ensure the validity and executability of the synthetically generated tasks.

A GPT-5 'teacher' model generates extensive agent trajectories by attempting to solve these tasks; these trajectories are then filtered for success and length to create a high-quality dataset for supervised fine-tuning of 'student' models.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Princeton University and Microsoft Research developed an unsupervised, scalable pipeline for generating synthetic machine learning tasks and rich, agentic interaction trajectories to train AI agents. Fine-tuning large language models on this data led to up to a 12% improvement in performance on the MLGym benchmark for complex machine learning research tasks.

title: AI Scientist via Synthetic Task Scaling

abstract: Agentic repository-level code understanding is essential for automating complex software engineering tasks, yet the field lacks reliable benchmarks. Existing evaluations often overlook the long tail topics and rely on popular repositories where Large Language Models (LLMs) can cheat via memorized knowledge. To address this, we introduce SWE-QA-Pro, a benchmark constructed from diverse, long-tail repositories with executable environments. We enforce topical balance via issue-driven clustering to cover under-represented task types and apply a rigorous difficulty calibration process: questions solvable by direct-answer baselines are filtered out. This results in a dataset where agentic workflows significantly outperform direct answering (e.g., a ~13-point gap for Claude Sonnet 4.5), confirming the necessity of agentic codebase exploration. Furthermore, to tackle the scarcity of training data for such complex behaviors, we propose a scalable synthetic data pipeline that powers a two-stage training recipe: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF). This approach allows small open models to learn efficient tool usage and reasoning. Empirically, a Qwen3-8B model trained with our recipe surpasses GPT-4o by 2.3 points on SWE-QA-Pro and substantially narrows the gap to state-of-the-art proprietary models, demonstrating both the validity of our evaluation and the effectiveness of our agentic training workflow.

keyInsights: The benchmark's difficulty calibration process effectively ensures that successful performance requires genuine codebase interaction and multi-step reasoning, validating the need for agentic capabilities.

Targeted SFT and RLAIF training can enable smaller open-source LLMs to develop strong agentic capabilities for repository-level QA, achieving competitive performance against or even surpassing larger proprietary models.

Tasks requiring causal/explanatory reasoning or involving configuration/workflow management proved significantly more challenging, demanding multi-file evidence integration and global semantic understanding.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM-assisted software engineering lack diversity, focusing on a limited number of popular projects and failing to cover a broad spectrum of semantic task categories.

Many current benchmark questions can be answered by LLMs relying on memorized pre-training knowledge, obscuring the models' true ability for active, grounded exploration and reasoning within an unfamiliar repository.
There is a scarcity of high-quality training data for teaching and reinforcing complex agentic behaviors and multi-step tool-use patterns in LLMs for repository-level contexts.
results: The SWE-QA-Pro benchmark revealed a substantial performance gap between direct-answer baselines and agentic models, with Claude Sonnet 4.5 showing a 13-point gap and GPT-4o a 6.5-point gap.

The Qwen3-8B model, trained with the SFT                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
The SWE-QA-Pro benchmark aims to test a model's ability to truly understand a codebase, rather than relying on memorized information. It introduces a challenging testbed and an effective training paradigm for LLM agents to actively reason and engage with real-world software repositories. This is supported by empirical evidence that the Qwen3-8B model, when trained with the SFT solution: Constructed SWE-QA-Pro, a benchmark that leverages issue-driven clustering for topical balance across 48 semantic categories and includes a difficulty calibration process to filter out questions solvable without repository interaction.
Developed a lightweight, ReAct-based SWE-QA-Pro Agent workflow using explicit, length-controlled tools (Search, View, CommandLine) for direct, iterative codebase exploration.
